title: A renewed push for tuberculosis control
html_body:
Biotechnology, drug discovery and pharma |
View this email in your browser Wednesday 8 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we hear how the newly-approved GLP-1 pill stacks up, consider how to maximize the absorption of water-insoluble drugs and explore a new approach to help drugs skip the blood-brain barrier altogether.
The drug and vaccine pipeline for one of humanity's oldest and deadliest infectious diseases is hotting up, after decades of stagnation. (NIAID/National Institutes of Health/Science Photo Library)

A renewed push for tuberculosis control

Almost 30 tuberculosis drugs are currently in clinical development, after decades of underinvestment. Other than during the COVID pandemic, tuberculosis is generally the leading cause of death from a single infectious disease — with lower- and middle-income communities carrying the heaviest burden. A phase III trial of the M72/AS01E vaccine is leading the pack of 16 vaccine candidates, and could help make global disease control a reality.

Nature Medicine | 11 min read

How the newly-okayed GLP-1 pill stacks up

Last week, the US Food and Drug Administration approved the second GLP-1 pill for weight loss, the fastest approval in decades. The once-daily orfoglipron pill resulted in an average of 12.2 kilograms of weight loss after 72 weeks of treatment in a clinical trial of 1613 overweight or obese people with type 2 diabetes; less than the already-approved tirzepatide. Another oral GLP-1 option could make GLP-1s more accessible compared to injectable treatments and drive down costs.

Scientific American | 5 min read

One drug, many viruses

An anti-viral pill has been found to inhibit a range of viruses, such as coronaviruses, respiratory syncytial virus, norovirus and influenza and hepatitis viruses. The drug, known as ERA-923, was originally developed for breast cancer, but showed little benefit in clinical trials. An AI drug-discovery platform suggested that the drug’s structure could help block viral replication, which has so far been backed up in lab studies. The hope is that the multipurpose drug could relieve unpleasant viral symptoms or possibly reduce the transmission of infections, in the case of another viral pandemic. Clinical trials are set to start next year.

New Scientist | 5 min read

Infographic of the week
Two processes have become the most common methods to help the absorption of oral drugs that are not very water-soluble: amorphous solid dispersions (ASDs) and lipid-based formulations (LBFs). ASDs blend the drug with a solvent that helps release the drug when it comes into contact with liquids, such as stomach acid. LBFs usually rely on dissolving the drug in a mixture of oils, surfactants — which help two liquids mix by reducing surface tension — and a solvent, and filling that mixture into a capsule. (Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 90 min read) (Ueda, K., Porter, C.J.H., Goodwin, A. et al./Nat. Rev. Drug Discov.)

Features and opinion

The PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway is back

New types of drugs and a better understanding of PI3K/AKT/mTOR signalling are renewing interest in the pathway as a drug target, write cancer and immunology researchers. The pathway acts inside the cell to regulate cell growth, metabolism, replication and survival. Its well-established role in cancer, immune regulation and genetic disorders — and its ability to be targeted fairly easily — have made it an exciting avenue for drug discovery. But so far, many drugs have struggled with side effects and drug resistance. Newer PI3Kδ inhibitors are showing promise. But fighting cancer, rather than simply stopping its progression, will likely involve using multiple inhibitors in tandem.

Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 110 min read

Can drugs skip the blood-brain barrier?

Researchers have skipped the blood-brain barrier altogether by injecting drug-loaded nanoparticles into the space between skull bone layers. Immune cells then ferried these nanoparticles into the brain. In a randomized study of 20 stroke patients, clinical outcomes were better in the group where treatment was also injected through the skull, compared to the standard of care. The ability to skip the blood-brain barrier in its entirety could be massively useful for treating many brain-based disorders, but there’s a lot more work in store, writes medicinal chemist Derek Lowe.

Science | 4 min read
 Reference: Cell paper

Biobusiness trend of the month
(Citeline Pharma R&D Annual Review 2026 (Source: Pharmaprojects, January 2026))

R&D shrinks for the first time in 30 years

A report by data analytics company Citeline suggests that the number of drugs in development has fallen for the first time since the mid-1990s. While changes in how Citeline collected the data might be partly responsible for the size of the fall, it likely means that the numbers plateaued earlier, the analysis suggests. It is unclear whether the new US administration and global unrest has restricted growth, which will eventually bounce back, or whether the sector will continue to wither, reads the report. (Fierce Biotech | 3 min read)
Reference: Citeline Pharma R&D Annual Review 2026

Quote of the week

“Sometimes you have to talk to the instruments or yell at them, and it’s much easier when they have names.”

Spatial transcriptomics research operations head Delissa McMillen says personifying lab equipment can help when they “get touchy”. The thermomixer — which rotates vials of liquid at various speeds — has garnered the nickname Shakira, for obvious reasons. (Nature Methods | 11 min read)

Bees also have hips that don’t lie, i.e. rhythm! Researchers have shown that buff-tailed bumblebees are able to recognise flexible, abstract rhythms: the first time this has been shown in such a small-brained (no offense intended) animal.

Please send your thoughts on whether bees like jazz — and your feedback — to trbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Laura Woodrow, editor, Nature Briefing: Translational Research
With contributions by Flora Graham

Clinical trials are set to start next year.

The pathway acts inside the cell to regulate cell growth, metabolism, replication and survival. summary.overview: 이번 네이처 브리핑은 결핵 통제 강화, GLP-1 제제 승인, 항바이러스 약물 개발, PI3K/AKT/mTOR 경로 재조명, 뇌혈관 장벽 우회 기술 등 주요 과학 이슈를 다룹니다.
covered_item_titles: A renewed push for tuberculosis control
How the newly-okayed GLP-1 pill stacks up
Can drugs skip the blood-brain barrier?

article_briefings.title: A renewed push for tuberculosis control
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 결핵 치료에 대한 투자 부족이 지속되던 중 30개 이상의 결핵 약물이 임상 개발 중이며, 이는 단일 감염병으로 인한 사망률 1위 질병인 결핵에 대한 새로운 대응 전략을 제시합니다.

article_briefings.title: How the newly-okayed GLP-1 pill stacks up
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 미국 FDA가 두 번째 GLP-1 제제를 승인하며, 72주간의 치료로 평균 12.2kg의 체중 감소를 기록한 orfoglipron의 빠른 승인 과정이 주목받고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: One drug, many viruses
article_briefings.briefing_ko: ERA-923라는 항바이러스 제제가 코로나바이러스, 인플루엔자, 간바이러스 등 다양한 바이러스를 억제할 수 있는 잠재력을 보여주며, 원래는 유방암 치료용으로 개발되었습니다.

article_briefings.title: The PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway is back
article_briefings.briefing_ko: PI3K/AKT/mTOR 경로가 세포 성장 및 대사 조절에 중요한 역할을 하며, 신약 개발과 신호 전달 이해 덕분에 암 및 면역학 분야에서 다시 주목받고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: Can drugs skip the blood-brain barrier?
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 약물이 뇌혈관 장벽을 우회하기 위해 나노입자 주사법을 사용해 면역 세포를 통해 뇌로 약물을 전달하는 기술이 20명의 중풍 환자에서 임상적 성공을 보였습니다.

raw_metadata.subject: A renewed push for tuberculosis control
